sentences.text: Good morning.
It's a pleasure to do it.
Yeah.
My name Zoltan Wieder, Z-O-L-T-A-N W-I-E-D-E-R.
My birth name was Zoltan Wieder, but it was spelled differently.
It was spelled with a V, V-I-D-E-R.
I did not.
Yes, my Hebrew name is Isroel.
And they used to call me, at home, when I was a child, S'ruku.
The Hebrew name?
Yeah, the Hebrew name I spelled [SPELLING IN YIDDISH]
The nickname, S-R-U-C-U.
No.
Not at all.
My birth date is 25 July, 1925.
I was born in Baia-Sprie.
That's in Romanian.
And in Hungarian, they called it Felsobanya.
Baia-Sprie?
B-A-I-A and S-P-R-I-E.
That was in Romania.
You're welcome.
Baia-Sprie was a small town.
Actually, it was a city.
It has about 5,000 population.
It was a small mining town.
The Jewish population were-- there were there about 300 people.
And we had only one synagogue.
And the most of the-- of the-- everybody knew everybody in that city.
It was a small town.
And the relations were very-- very good between the people.
Yeah, they mined there gold, silver, iron, and zinc and chrome.
Baia-Sprie is-- is in the northern part of Romania, which is Transylvania.
It's a province of Romania.
It's actually the northern part of Transylvania.
It's very close-- used to be the-- the prior border was Czechoslovakia.
And now it's Ukraine.
It's about 50 miles from Ukraine.
It's Sighet, which is-- which is right the border town of Ukraine.
It's-- in Romania, you call it Sighetu Marmatiei.
That's-- it's about 35 miles from Baia-Sprie.
The mines were right in the town of Baia-Sprie.
It-- right, actually, not in the center, but in the northern part of the town.
Yeah, my parents always lived in Baia-Sprie.
My father was born in Baia-Sprie.
My father's name was Samuel Wieder.
And my mother's name was Regina.
Her maiden name was Mendel.
She was born in another town called, in Romanian, [ROMANIAN] or in Hungarian, [HUNGARIAN].
That was about-- the-- about 20 miles from Zalau, which was the biggest town near there.
And it was about 50, 60 miles from Baia-Sprie, south of Baia-Sprie.
My father--
--he had a-- a general store, a small store.
And I know I-- I was born in '25.
So I heard, you know, in '29 when it was the depression here, they had big problems there too.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH] And he lost-- because they sold in the general store based on-- on credit.
You know, people paid once a month.
And they lost a lot of money because they couldn't pay.
So he struggled after that with-- with his store.
He didn't have enough money to buy merchandise.
And he had to go to work also, beside, in-- in the forest industry.
He worked for the-- for a company.
He was-- What did he do for--
He was managing-- he was a-- a forest technician also.
So he worked-- he was managing people in the work, organize the work, and so on.
They-- they cut trees in the-- in the forest.
And they-- and they produced wood.
And because everybody was there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cooking with-- with-- or-- or heating the homes with wood.
They were all wood-burn stones-- stoves.
And beside, they produce also coal, coal which was used for the steel industry.
The Jewish population, in general, there were about 300 people, like I said, including the children.
They were pretty poor, most of them.
There were a few families in better conditions, some middle class.
Like I say, my-- my family was also some middle-class people.
But they were struggling.
They were working very hard.
And some, they had some stores.
Others, they worked in different professions-- carpenters, tailors.
They-- some, they worked in agriculture.
Some, they worked with-- they had horses and little wagons.
And that was, in general, the work what they did.
The Jews lived-- some of them lived in the middle of the town, in the center of the town, actually, because it was one main street.
The whole town was a big main-- a long main street.
And there were some small streets besides.
Some, they lived on that main street.
And some, they lived in different small streets.
Pardon me?
Yeah, we lived in-- in the center of the town, but on a-- on a-- not on that main street, on a-- on a secondary street.
Yeah, I recall very vividly.
Our house was built right on-- on a little river.
They called it Sasar in Romanian, or-- or [HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian.
And we looked from the window, and we had the river right-- right on our-- right near our house.
And we had the store in front of the-- in front of the house.
The store in the house.
In the house.
Part of the house, the store, a small store.
And we had our bedroom there.
And we had a living room.
And we had another room where we-- I-- that was our room, my-- my brothers and myself.
We were four children.
My brother, myself--
My brother's name was Adolf.
And his Hebrew name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was four years older than me.
He was born in 1921, May 10th.
And I had two sisters.
My one sister, her-- her name-- well, she was two years younger than me.
Her Hebrew name was-- Yiddish name-- Blima.
And her official name was Wilhelmina.
And I had a smaller sister.
She was four years younger than me.
She was born in '29.
Her name was Khana.
And Anna in-- in Hungarian or Romanian.
And we were living-- we were living in that-- we had only, actually, two rooms beside the living room.
And it was pretty crowded.
And the bathroom was outside.
Not-- we didn't have inside.
We had running water and-- at that time.
And I remember, we had there, if somebody needed the bathroom, they had to go out.
Yes, we had running water.
Yeah, we had electricity too.
That was our own home, yeah.
And that was my father got it from his father and-- and helping.
And-- and he built it up a little bit.
And so that was our own home.
It-- it was considered a-- a pretty big house there, you know, at that time.
And we had a yard in the home.
Yeah, we had flowers.
And beautiful-- my-- my mother loved flowers.
And she worked all the time with her flowers.
We had-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and we did, because there were wood stoves there, so my brother, myself, we always chopped wood.
Yeah, we had-- we have-- we had cats.
And we had a dog.
But there you kept the dog in the-- in the yard.
And we had two, three cats all the time.
There it wasn't like here-- you have a Jewish area or not.
That was-- you know, there were some Jews there, around there.
There were some other people around there, gentile people, Hungarians, because it was a-- there were-- this town was mostly Hungarians.
And the Romanian-- there lived some Romanian peoples too.
But the Romanians mostly around the-- around the town in small-- and other religious.
But most of the townspeople were Hungarians, actually.
We-- we had with the population-- we had very good relationships in the beginning.
And potentially started when-- in 19-- it was still Romania in 1938 when the-- when the Guardists came, the-- the Iron Guard came to power in Romania.
It was a lot-- a lot of antisemitism therein.
And I remember, in 1938, they-- as soon as they came to power, they gave-- they gave out a decree.
We have to open the store in Shabbat.
So that was a very big thing because my parents were religious.
And so they had to open.
And-- and it was only one week because that government fall after two weeks.
And the King Carol-- Carol was the king of Romania at that time.
He took over the government at that time.
And so they fell.
But it was a lot of anti-Semitism.
And I remember my teachers was a family-- [NON-ENGLISH].
That was their name.
And he was a [NON-ENGLISH].
That was also the-- from-- connected with Iron Guard.
But he was nicer to us because we grew up there.
They grew up there.
But I remember he went to rallies, to Cluj and other parts, you know.
And-- and they-- and at rallies, they incited the people and against the Jews, and so on.
Yes, I had non-Jewish friends in the school.
And we were very good friends.
We were good friends.
And I remember I had a very good friend.
And he was in the same school with me.
And when the-- when they took us out from-- from-- to the ghetto from-- from our town with the trucks, he didn't-- I saw him.
He didn't look at me.
So he changed completely.
And some of them, they did change, you know, at that time.
But in the beginning, I had-- in the school, we had very good relations.
I had a lot of friends, non-Jewish too.
But I had-- my best friends were Jewish boys.
And--
Oh, yeah.
Non-Jewish homes?
Yes, we did visit non-Jewish homes too.
And see, we had-- this was a small town.
I had relatives there.
I had my two uncles, two aunts there that were living there, my grandfather, who died in '36.
So I knew him when I was a small child.
Yeah, I remember he was-- he came-- he left-- he-- he was living next-- next house to us.
And every morning he was coming.
And to my younger sister, he was bringing cake.
And he was widower, because I didn't know-- I didn't know my grandmother.
She died before I was born.
And he had a housekeeper.
And-- and she-- she baked.
And he always brought, in the morning, cake.
And--
I went to-- [COUGHING] I went to the school that was only one school in the town, public school, naturally.
And it was in the middle of the town.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH], a primary school.
It was a primary school, [NON-ENGLISH] Baia-Sprie.
And I did there seven years, for seven years, including junior high-- eight years-- seven years.
There were seven, actually, there.
The teachers were the Romanians.
And some Hungarian teachers also.
But they teach in the Romanian school.
And they were nice, very nice.
And I remember we-- we had some exams when it was in '38, in that time.
And they put some exams on-- on a Saturday, on a Shabbat.
And it was a big problem for me to go in and do the exams in Shabbes and write Shabbes.
And one of the teachers, who was my teacher actually-- and she let me out to give the exam on another day.
So there were--
Me-- yeah, other Jewish boys also--
Boys and girls.
Coed, coed.
Yeah, in the same class, yeah.
Jewish, there were a few.
Could be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe-- maybe 5%.
No, no Jewish teachers at all.
In my house, they spoke Hungarian because my parents grew up in Hungary.
At that time, it was Hungary, before-- before 1918.
So they grew up in there.
Their school's done in-- in Hungarian.
And we spoke Yiddish too.
So we spoke Hungarian and Yiddish in the house.
Everybody spoke Yiddish, my-- my brother, my sisters.
And--
But-- or, actually, my native language was Hungarian, actually.
In the school we spoke Romanian.
In the school, we were dressed short pants because you didn't get long pants until you were 13, bar mitzvah.
Boys.
Jewish-- no, everybody wore--
All the children wore short pants.
Children below 12 or 13 years old, everybody wore short pants.
And in the-- in the winter, we had some-- they called it some English pants that-- golf pants, like, they call-- golf pants, like they are here.
And but we didn't have long pants when we were children.
And we didn't have uniforms.
But everybody was dressed, you know, OK.
No, they didn't allow this.
No, I didn't have my head-- we-- we didn't-- we didn't wear Yarmulkes there.
Not hats in the school.
My father was dressed regular business suit, a regular suit.
And especially in the-- in the store, he was in a working suit.
And he had a hat or a cap.
And we wear caps actually, not in the school, but on the street.
And when-- when we were younger, we wore caps.
My mother, she was dressed also.
She-- you know, she helped in the store.
But she was also-- you know, we were four children.
She was doing the housekeeping and the cleaning and everything else.
And she was very good cook.
And that-- Friday morning, when I woke up, she was already cooking Danish and all kind of cakes and challah.
She made the challah.
She was doing it.
And-- and we had a bakery right near our house.
So we took there the challah.
And they baked it for Shabbes and the [NON-ENGLISH].
And during the week, Monday she cooked bread.
She baked bread.
She had a variety of big bread.
And they-- we took it to the bakery.
They did it there.
And-- and then the bread-- we took it back home.
We had bread for a whole week.
A big bread.
It was tremendous, such a big bread.
We had this-- we had it for a whole week.
It was a white-- white, brownish, you know, because everybody was buying flour.
We sold flour too in the store.
And they took it home.
And they made bread with-- with potatoes.
They put in potatoes at that time.
And no eggs in the bread.
The challah, they did.
And we had always, Tuesday morning, beginning Tuesday morning, fresh bread.
We had also like a pizza type-- they did it.
They called it langos, which-- which was a-- like a pita.
Mhm.
Flat-- like a flat bread.
Flat, flat, yeah.
Oh, we loved it.
Tuesday morning, we got that fresh one.
We loved it.
Yes, she had a [NON-ENGLISH].
She had a-- a sheitel, a wig.
She wear the wig.
But otherwise she had covered her head.
In the school games?
I tell you, every hour we had a few-- we had 5-- 10 minutes we had recreation there.
We went out to the yard.
And we played-- we played soccer.
We did some gym, some gymnastics.
But it was nothing with-- you didn't have a room, gymnastic room or something.
Even-- even to get a ball was a big thing there in the school, you know, to play.
So we played in the-- in the yard there for 10 minutes, and we went back to classes.
Discipline was great, you know?
You couldn't-- you looked up to your teacher like you were looking to-- to-- to God.
You know, nobody was-- had a word in the school.
And-- and-- and even-- there were corporal punishment.
If somebody was bad, they were-- they were hitting them.
They-- they got hit.
But discipline was-- everybody was, you know, listening to the teacher.
And--
--there were no talking back or something.
Yeah, we played-- we played in groups, or sometimes together.
And some people had different types of-- some played soccer.
The girls didn't, you know.
So they played something else, you know.
Actually--
In school.
Or on the way home and back.
Yeah, I had sometimes.
They called me a Jew or something, you know?
But very little there in the town until 1940 when the Hungarian came in.
Then it started big antisemitism.
But before-- because we-- we knew each other and-- and we were really friends, considered friends.
And-- and we didn't have too much.
We had in other places.
Like when I went to [ROMANIAN], to Cluj, that-- when they-- you know, they came after me or they tried to-- to hit me, or whatever, you know?
And-- and screaming, 'Jew,' 'dirty Jew,' whatever, you know.
That was between '40 and '44.
Yeah.
The kehillah, it was-- the Jewish kehillah was-- it was an Orthodox kehillah.
It was a nice synagogue, a pretty big one.
And the women were upstairs and-- and the men downstairs.
[COUGHS] They had the ritual bath in the-- [COUGHS] --in the yard.
And we went to, every morning and evening-- actually, we went to school, we couldn't go.
We went-- they woke us up early morning.
We went to the cheder to-- we were down there in the morning.
And then we went to school.
The public-- the public school started at 8 o'clock.
So they woke us up about 6 o'clock.
And we went to-- as young little children, we went to the cheder.
To the cheder, we--
Yeah, yeah.
Only the boys, not the girls.
And from there, we went to the school, and until 1 o'clock.
School was from 8:00 to 1:00.
And at 1 o'clock, we went home.
Everybody was eating lunch.
Lunch was the main, the main course.
That was the dinner, actually, at 1 o'clock.
And everybody was sitting down to the table, the children.
My father, if he was out in the-- in the forest-- he went out sometimes 20 miles or 10 miles, you know, to work.
And he had his job-- or when he wasn't in the store.
So otherwise, everybody was eating at 1 o'clock together.
We had lunch.
And at 2 o'clock, we went-- I went to the cheder back.
And I was there until about 6 o'clock.
And then I went home.
And I-- I had to do my homework in the evening.
And in the evening, we had only a cup of coffee or some bread with water, something like this.
It was a light food in the evening.
For the main dinner-- the main lunch was my mother cooked.
And she had pretty-- pretty well-established.
Like, Monday we had always dairy, dairy.
We had soup.
We had-- we had sour cream soup.
And we had pasta with cheese.
Or we had blintzes.
And that was always Monday.
And Tuesday she did some other vegetable soup and-- and some pasta or something.
And Wednesday, the same thing.
And then Thursday she did-- that was a-- beans.
There was a bean soup and, of course, some beans made up with some-- with some hamburgers, right?
She made hamburgers too.
Actually, meat we had sometimes Thursday a little bit.
But we had-- for Saturday we had meat.
She bought meat from the butcher shop.
And-- and we had some chicken.
And she cooked for Shabbes.
Shabbes we had a better-- a better supper.
We had always a Friday night supper.
And-- and she had chicken soup.
She had meat.
She had, like, gefilte fish, but that was not from-- she was doing it from meat.
It was a false-- they called it false fish.
And it was very good.
